front: What tone wins in strategic competitive conversations—not tribal dismissal?
back: Curious, specific, evidence-seeking.

front: Give three discovery questions from the module for competitive situations.
back: What workflow is the team optimizing for? What did the last evaluation measure—speed, quality, security controls, adoption? Who decided “good enough”—engineering, security, or procurement?

front: Anchor differentiation in three things—not a feature checklist meme.
back: Workflow depth, enterprise controls, how evaluations are actually won in practice.

front: One-line anchor for GitHub Copilot—what is it optimized for?
back: Deep GitHub workflow integration; bundling power; strong default for GitHub-centric shops (hypothesis—validate in account).

front: One-line anchor for Claude Code / terminal-native agents?
back: Strong autonomy story for engineers who live in CLI workflows (hypothesis—validate).

front: JetBrains ecosystem competitor—what’s the angle?
back: IDE-native depth for teams standardized on JetBrains.

front: Amazon Q Developer—what buyer alignment does the module call out?
back: AWS-centric buyer alignment and packaging dynamics.

front: For Windsurf and “others”—what should you ask instead of debating hype?
back: What did the team actually try—and what broke in real repos?

front: “We already have Copilot via GitHub.” Name four discovery angles from the module.
back: Where multi-file work stalls; how review handles agentic changes; what controls are required; what developers choose when they can pick.

front: What three elements make a battle card useful—not “why we win”?
back: Trigger (when competitor appears), landmine questions, proof that closes doubt (POC design, security artifacts, references).

front: Separate “feature parity” talk from what three realities?
back: Workflow fit, governance fit, and preference/reality of how teams evaluate and adopt.

front: Why are one-line competitor anchors “starting points, not scripture”?
back: You validate hypotheses in discovery—accounts differ; stale positioning loses credibility.

front: Why is insulting a competitor the buyer’s team uses a credibility burn?
back: It signals you can’t win on substance and insults the buyer’s judgment for choosing it in the first place.

front: Why must a competitive POC mirror the buyer’s real workflow?
back: Because polished generic demos don’t predict how a tool behaves on their repo, with their reviewers, on their constraints.

front: What single question keeps a POC from becoming political instead of evidentiary?
back: “What outcome would we both call ‘success’ on this POC, and how would we know?”

front: Define ‘bundling power’ as a competitive dynamic.
back: Vendors offering products inside a larger contract or platform suite, lowering perceived incremental cost—even when the standalone tool is weaker.

front: Why anchor your story in workflow + governance + preference instead of feature claims?
back: Public competitor specs drift; workflow depth, governance, and developer preference outlast the spec sheet.

front: Why does ‘tribal’ competitive talk hurt with senior engineering leaders?
back: They live in the same category—tribalism reads as insecurity and erodes the trust you need for a strategic deal.

front: How do you separate “good demo” from “good evaluation” for the buyer?
back: Push toward an in-repo, in-workflow comparison run by their reviewers—demos are theater, evaluations are evidence.

front: How should you treat a self-described “JetBrains shop” in a Cursor pursuit?
back: Probe—engineers cross IDEs, repos, and tools constantly; ask where engineers actually leave JetBrains today.

front: AWS-aligned account—how do you test if Q Developer is truly the default?
back: Ask how procurement is structured, who decided ‘good enough,’ and what workflows actually run inside the AWS console.

front: When the buyer is GitHub-centric, the strongest workflow questions are about?
back: How multi-file work, review depth, and governance for agentic changes are handled today inside that workflow.

front: When a buyer wants a feature matrix and refuses to discuss workflow, what does that signal?
back: A checklist evaluation—reframe toward a workflow-anchored POC whenever you can; checklists rarely correlate with how teams actually choose.

front: How do you separate real preference signal from rumor?
back: Ask how preference was measured—opt-in usage, structured evaluation, surveys—over what time horizon, and by whom.

front: What kind of reference call actually moves a competitive deal?
back: One the buyer trusts (similar org, similar workflow), not just any logo on a slide.
